sectionTitle: Diagonalization, Projections, Components, and Cross Product
course: 21-254 Vector Calculus and Linear Algebra for Engineers
contentPolicyNote: Original SI-style problems inspired by the uploaded course calendar, notes, and practice exams. Do not copy official homework or exam wording. Solutions are independently worked out and should be reviewed before publishing.
studentFacingDescription: Practice diagonalization, powers of diagonalizable matrices, eigenvector-based iteration, vector projections/components, cross products, planes, areas, and scalar triple products in the style of the Exam 2 material.

recommendedFlow: Read the problem and try it on paper first.
Reveal hints one at a time.
Reveal the setup before the full solution.
Reveal the full worked solution and common mistakes.

problems.unit: Diagonalization, Projections, Components, and Cross Product
problems.title: Diagonalize a Matrix and Compute a Power
skills: Use diagonalization to compute a matrix power
problems.examFlavor: Standard diagonalization problem with a matrix power follow-up
problems.problemLatex:
Let
\[A=\begin{bmatrix}3&2\\0&1\end{bmatrix}.\]

(a) Find a diagonalization \(A=PDP^{-1}\).

(b) Use your diagonalization to compute \(A^5\).
problems.thinkFirst: Do not multiply the matrix by itself five times. Diagonalization is useful because powers of diagonal matrices are easy.
hints: Since \(A\) is triangular, its eigenvalues are the diagonal entries.
Find one eigenvector for each eigenvalue, then use those eigenvectors as the columns of \(P\).
problems.setupLatex: The eigenvalues are \(3\) and \(1\). Since they are distinct, the matrix should be diagonalizable over \(\mathbb{R}\).

solutionSteps.label: Find the eigenvectors
solutionSteps.latex:
For \(\lambda=3\),
\[A-3I=\begin{bmatrix}0&2\\0&-2\end{bmatrix},\]
so \(y=0\), and one eigenvector is
\[v_1=\begin{bmatrix}1\\0\end{bmatrix}.\]

For \(\lambda=1\),
\[A-I=\begin{bmatrix}2&2\\0&0\end{bmatrix},\]
so \(x+y=0\). One eigenvector is
\[v_2=\begin{bmatrix}-1\\1\end{bmatrix}.\]

solutionSteps.label: Build \(P\) and \(D\)
solutionSteps.latex:
Use the eigenvectors as the columns of \(P\), in the same order as the eigenvalues in \(D\):
\[P=\begin{bmatrix}1&-1\\0&1\end{bmatrix},\qquad D=\begin{bmatrix}3&0\\0&1\end{bmatrix}.\]
Then
\[A=PDP^{-1}.\]

solutionSteps.label: Compute the fifth power
solutionSteps.latex:
First,
\[D^5=\begin{bmatrix}3^5&0\\0&1^5\end{bmatrix}=\begin{bmatrix}243&0\\0&1\end{bmatrix}.\]
Also,
\[P^{-1}=\begin{bmatrix}1&1\\0&1\end{bmatrix}.\]
So
\[A^5=PD^5P^{-1}=\begin{bmatrix}1&-1\\0&1\end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix}243&0\\0&1\end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix}1&1\\0&1\end{bmatrix}
=\begin{bmatrix}243&242\\0&1\end{bmatrix}.\]

commonMistakes: Putting the eigenvalues in a different order than the corresponding eigenvectors in \(P\).
Computing \(P^5D^5(P^{-1})^5\) instead of \(PD^5P^{-1}\).
Forgetting that a triangular matrix has eigenvalues on the diagonal.
problems.similarProblemPrompt: Try the same problem for \(A=\begin{bmatrix}4&3\\0&2\end{bmatrix}\), then compute \(A^4\).

problems.unit: Diagonalization, Projections, Components, and Cross Product
problems.title: A Repeated Eigenvalue That Still Diagonalizes
problems.examFlavor: Conceptual diagonalization problem with a repeated eigenvalue
problems.problemLatex:
Let
\[A=\begin{bmatrix}2&0&0\\0&3&1\\0&1&3\end{bmatrix}.\]

(a) Find the eigenvalues of \(A\), including their algebraic multiplicities.

(b) Find bases for the eigenspaces.

(c) Decide whether \(A\) is diagonalizable. If it is, give matrices \(P\) and \(D\) such that \(A=PDP^{-1}\).
problems.thinkFirst: A repeated eigenvalue is not automatically bad. The key question is whether the repeated eigenvalue has enough independent eigenvectors.
hints: The first coordinate is separated from the bottom-right \(2\times2\) block.
For \(\lambda=2\), solve \((A-2I)x=0\), not just one equation from the characteristic polynomial.
A \(3\times3\) matrix is diagonalizable if you can find three linearly independent eigenvectors.
problems.setupLatex: Because \(A\) is block triangular, the characteristic polynomial can be read from the \(1\times1\) block \([2]\) and the bottom-right block \(\begin{bmatrix}3&1\\1&3\end{bmatrix}\).

solutionSteps.label: Find the characteristic polynomial
solutionSteps.latex:
Using \(p_A(\lambda)=\det(\lambda I-A)\),
\[p_A(\lambda)=(\lambda-2)\det\begin{bmatrix}\lambda-3&-1\\-1&\lambda-3\end{bmatrix}.\]
Thus
\[p_A(\lambda)=(\lambda-2)((\lambda-3)^2-1)=(\lambda-2)^2(\lambda-4).\]
So \(\lambda=2\) has algebraic multiplicity \(2\), and \(\lambda=4\) has algebraic multiplicity \(1\).

solutionSteps.label: Find the eigenspace for \(\lambda=2\)
solutionSteps.latex:
For \(\lambda=2\),
\[A-2I=\begin{bmatrix}0&0&0\\0&1&1\\0&1&1\end{bmatrix}.\]
The equation is \(y+z=0\), while \(x\) is free. Therefore
\[E_2=\operatorname{span}\left\{\begin{bmatrix}1\\0\\0\end{bmatrix},\begin{bmatrix}0\\1\\-1\end{bmatrix}\right\}.\]

solutionSteps.label: Find the eigenspace for \(\lambda=4\)
solutionSteps.latex:
For \(\lambda=4\),
\[A-4I=\begin{bmatrix}-2&0&0\\0&-1&1\\0&1&-1\end{bmatrix}.\]
The equations give \(x=0\) and \(y=z\). Thus
\[E_4=\operatorname{span}\left\{\begin{bmatrix}0\\1\\1\end{bmatrix}\right\}.\]

solutionSteps.label: Diagonalize
solutionSteps.latex:
The eigenspace for \(\lambda=2\) has dimension \(2\), matching its algebraic multiplicity. Together with the eigenvector for \(\lambda=4\), we have three independent eigenvectors.

So \(A\) is diagonalizable. One valid choice is
\[P=\begin{bmatrix}1&0&0\\0&1&1\\0&-1&1\end{bmatrix},\qquad D=\begin{bmatrix}2&0&0\\0&2&0\\0&0&4\end{bmatrix}.\]

commonMistakes: Assuming a repeated eigenvalue means the matrix is automatically not diagonalizable.
Forgetting that the order of columns in \(P\) must match the order of diagonal entries in \(D\).
Only finding one eigenvector for \(\lambda=2\) and stopping too early.
problems.similarProblemPrompt: Try changing the bottom-right block to \(\begin{bmatrix}3&2\\0&3\end{bmatrix}\) and compare what happens to diagonalizability.

problems.unit: Diagonalization, Projections, Components, and Cross Product
problems.title: A Repeated Eigenvalue That Does Not Diagonalize
problems.examFlavor: Diagonalizability decision with a repeated eigenvalue
problems.problemLatex:
Let
\[A=\begin{bmatrix}4&1&0\\0&4&0\\0&0&2\end{bmatrix}.\]

(a) Find the eigenvalues and their algebraic multiplicities.

(b) Find the dimension of each eigenspace.

(c) Is \(A\) diagonalizable? Explain.
problems.thinkFirst: The diagonal entries tell you the eigenvalues, but diagonalizability depends on eigenspace dimensions, not just eigenvalue values.
hints: Because \(A\) is triangular, the eigenvalues are on the diagonal.
For \(\lambda=4\), solve \((A-4I)x=0\) and count the number of free variables.
A \(3\times3\) matrix needs three linearly independent eigenvectors to be diagonalizable.
problems.setupLatex: The eigenvalue \(4\) is repeated. We need to compare its algebraic multiplicity with the dimension of its eigenspace.

solutionSteps.label: Use triangular form for the eigenvalues
solutionSteps.latex:
Since \(A\) is upper triangular, its eigenvalues are the diagonal entries:
\[\lambda=4,4,2.\]
So \(\lambda=4\) has algebraic multiplicity \(2\), and \(\lambda=2\) has algebraic multiplicity \(1\).

solutionSteps.label: Find the eigenspace for \(\lambda=4\)
solutionSteps.latex:
For \(\lambda=4\),
\[A-4I=\begin{bmatrix}0&1&0\\0&0&0\\0&0&-2\end{bmatrix}.\]
The equations are \(y=0\) and \(z=0\), with \(x\) free. Therefore
\[E_4=\operatorname{span}\left\{\begin{bmatrix}1\\0\\0\end{bmatrix}\right\},\]
so \(\dim(E_4)=1\).

solutionSteps.label: Find the eigenspace for \(\lambda=2\)
solutionSteps.latex:
For \(\lambda=2\),
\[A-2I=\begin{bmatrix}2&1&0\\0&2&0\\0&0&0\end{bmatrix}.\]
The equations force \(y=0\), then \(x=0\), while \(z\) is free. Thus
\[E_2=\operatorname{span}\left\{\begin{bmatrix}0\\0\\1\end{bmatrix}\right\},\]
so \(\dim(E_2)=1\).

solutionSteps.label: Decide diagonalizability
solutionSteps.latex:
The total number of independent eigenvectors is
\[\dim(E_4)+\dim(E_2)=1+1=2.\]
A \(3\times3\) matrix needs \(3\) linearly independent eigenvectors to be diagonalizable, so \(A\) is not diagonalizable.

problems.finalAnswerLatex: \(A\) is not diagonalizable because \(\lambda=4\) has algebraic multiplicity \(2\) but geometric multiplicity \(1\).
commonMistakes: Saying the matrix is diagonalizable just because all eigenvalues are visible on the diagonal.
Confusing algebraic multiplicity with geometric multiplicity.
Forgetting to count the total number of independent eigenvectors.
problems.similarProblemPrompt: Try replacing the entry \(1\) above the repeated \(4\) by \(0\). Does the matrix become diagonalizable?

problems.unit: Diagonalization, Projections, Components, and Cross Product
problems.title: Use Eigenvectors to Compute \(A^n x_0\)
skills: Use an eigenbasis without explicitly knowing \(A\)
Write a vector as a linear combination of eigenvectors
Compute repeated matrix multiplication using eigenvalues
problems.problemLatex:
Suppose \(A\) is a \(2\times2\) matrix with eigenvectors
\[u=\begin{bmatrix}1\\1\end{bmatrix},\qquad v=\begin{bmatrix}1\\-1\end{bmatrix},\]
where \(Au=5u\) and \(Av=-v\). Let
\[x_0=\begin{bmatrix}3\\1\end{bmatrix}.\]

(a) Write \(x_0\) as a linear combination of \(u\) and \(v\).

(b) Find a formula for \(A^n x_0\).

(c) Compute \(A^3x_0\).
problems.thinkFirst: If a vector is written in an eigenbasis, applying \(A\) only scales each eigenvector component.
problems.setupLatex: The vectors \(u\) and \(v\) are linearly independent, so they form a basis for \(\mathbb{R}^2\).

solutionSteps.label: Write \(x_0\) in the eigenbasis
solutionSteps.latex:
We want
\[\begin{bmatrix}3\\1\end{bmatrix}=c_1\begin{bmatrix}1\\1\end{bmatrix}+c_2\begin{bmatrix}1\\-1\end{bmatrix}.\]
This gives
\[c_1+c_2=3,\qquad c_1-c_2=1.\]
Adding the equations gives \(2c_1=4\), so \(c_1=2\). Then \(c_2=1\). Therefore
\[x_0=2u+v.\]

solutionSteps.label: Apply \(A^n\)
solutionSteps.latex:
Using the eigenvalue scaling rules,
\[A^nx_0=A^n(2u+v)=2A^nu+A^nv.\]
So
\[A^nx_0=2\cdot5^n u+(-1)^n v.\]

solutionSteps.label: Evaluate at \(n=3\)
solutionSteps.latex:
When \(n=3\),
\[A^3x_0=2\cdot5^3\begin{bmatrix}1\\1\end{bmatrix}+(-1)^3\begin{bmatrix}1\\-1\end{bmatrix}.\]
Thus
\[A^3x_0=250\begin{bmatrix}1\\1\end{bmatrix}-\begin{bmatrix}1\\-1\end{bmatrix}=\begin{bmatrix}249\\251\end{bmatrix}.\]

commonMistakes: Trying to find the actual matrix \(A\) even though the eigenbasis information is enough.
Forgetting that the negative eigenvalue becomes \((-1)^n\).
Writing \(A^n(2u+v)=2A^nu+A^nv\) correctly but then forgetting the coefficients.
problems.similarProblemPrompt: Try the same question with eigenvalues \(2\) and \(3\), and initial vector \(x_0=\begin{bmatrix}5\\-1\end{bmatrix}\).

problems.unit: Diagonalization, Projections, Components, and Cross Product
problems.title: Projection Onto a Vector and Orthogonal Decomposition
skills: Split a vector into parallel and perpendicular components
Check orthogonality with a dot product
problems.problemLatex:
Let
\[a=\begin{bmatrix}2\\-1\\2\end{bmatrix},\qquad b=\begin{bmatrix}5\\1\\0\end{bmatrix}.\]

(a) Find \(\operatorname{proj}_a(b)\), the projection of \(b\) onto the line spanned by \(a\).

(b) Write \(b=b_{\parallel}+b_{\perp}\), where \(b_{\parallel}\) is parallel to \(a\) and \(b_{\perp}\) is perpendicular to \(a\).

(c) Verify that your perpendicular component is actually perpendicular to \(a\).
problems.thinkFirst: Projection is the parallel part. The perpendicular part is what remains after subtracting the projection from the original vector.
problems.setupLatex: We will compute the scalar factor \(\frac{b\cdot a}{a\cdot a}\) first.

solutionSteps.label: Find the projection
solutionSteps.latex:
Therefore
\[\operatorname{proj}_a(b)=\frac{b\cdot a}{a\cdot a}a=\frac{9}{9}a=a=\begin{bmatrix}2\\-1\\2\end{bmatrix}.\]
So
\[b_{\parallel}=\begin{bmatrix}2\\-1\\2\end{bmatrix}.\]

solutionSteps.label: Find the perpendicular component
solutionSteps.latex:
Now subtract:
\[b_{\perp}=b-b_{\parallel}=\begin{bmatrix}5\\1\\0\end{bmatrix}-\begin{bmatrix}2\\-1\\2\end{bmatrix}=\begin{bmatrix}3\\2\\-2\end{bmatrix}.\]

solutionSteps.label: Check perpendicularity
solutionSteps.latex:
Finally,
\[a\cdot b_{\perp}=\begin{bmatrix}2\\-1\\2\end{bmatrix}\cdot\begin{bmatrix}3\\2\\-2\end{bmatrix}=6-2-4=0.\]
So \(b_{\perp}\) is perpendicular to \(a\).

commonMistakes: Using \(b\cdot b\) in the denominator instead of \(a\cdot a\).
Forgetting that the projection is a vector, not just a scalar.
Calling \(b-b_{\parallel}\) parallel instead of perpendicular.

problems.unit: Diagonalization, Projections, Components, and Cross Product
problems.title: Closest Point on a Line Through the Origin
skills: Compute distance using the perpendicular component
problems.problemLatex:
Let \(L\) be the line through the origin spanned by
\[u=\begin{bmatrix}1\\2\\2\end{bmatrix}.\]
Let
\[p=\begin{bmatrix}4\\1\\3\end{bmatrix}.\]

(a) Find the point on \(L\) closest to \(p\).

(b) Find the distance from \(p\) to \(L\).
problems.thinkFirst: The closest point on a line through the origin is the projection of the point vector onto the direction vector of the line.
problems.setupLatex: Because the line goes through the origin, the point on \(L\) closest to \(p\) is just the vector projection of \(p\) onto \(u\).

solutionSteps.label: Compute the projection coefficient
solutionSteps.latex:
Compute
\[p\cdot u=4(1)+1(2)+3(2)=12,\]
and
\[u\cdot u=1^2+2^2+2^2=9.\]
Therefore the projection coefficient is
\[\frac{p\cdot u}{u\cdot u}=\frac{12}{9}=\frac43.\]

solutionSteps.label: Find the closest point
solutionSteps.latex:
Thus
\[\operatorname{proj}_u(p)=\frac43\begin{bmatrix}1\\2\\2\end{bmatrix}=\begin{bmatrix}4/3\\8/3\\8/3\end{bmatrix}.\]
This is the closest point on \(L\) to \(p\).

solutionSteps.label: Find the perpendicular vector
solutionSteps.latex:
The vector from the closest point to \(p\) is
\[p-\operatorname{proj}_u(p)=\begin{bmatrix}4\\1\\3\end{bmatrix}-\begin{bmatrix}4/3\\8/3\\8/3\end{bmatrix}=\begin{bmatrix}8/3\\-5/3\\1/3\end{bmatrix}.\]

solutionSteps.label: Compute the distance
solutionSteps.latex:
Therefore
\[\operatorname{dist}(p,L)=\left\|\begin{bmatrix}8/3\\-5/3\\1/3\end{bmatrix}\right\|=\frac13\sqrt{64+25+1}=\frac{\sqrt{90}}3=\sqrt{10}.\]

problems.finalAnswerLatex: The closest point is \(\begin{bmatrix}4/3\\8/3\\8/3\end{bmatrix}\), and the distance is \(\sqrt{10}\).
commonMistakes: Using a point on the line other than the projection as the closest point.
Stopping after finding the projection and forgetting to compute the distance.
Using \(\|\operatorname{proj}_u(p)\|\) as the distance instead of the perpendicular component length.
problems.similarProblemPrompt: Try finding the distance from \(p=\begin{bmatrix}2\\-1\\5\end{bmatrix}\) to the line spanned by \(u=\begin{bmatrix}0\\1\\2\end{bmatrix}\).

problems.unit: Diagonalization, Projections, Components, and Cross Product
problems.title: Scalar and Vector Components Along a Direction
skills: Find vector components parallel and perpendicular to a direction
problems.problemLatex:
A force vector is
\[F=\begin{bmatrix}10\\0\end{bmatrix}.\]
A direction is given by
\[d=\begin{bmatrix}3\\4\end{bmatrix}.\]

(a) Find the scalar component of \(F\) in the direction of \(d\).

(b) Find the vector component of \(F\) parallel to \(d\).

(c) Find the vector component of \(F\) perpendicular to \(d\).
problems.thinkFirst: Scalar component uses a unit direction vector. Vector component is the projection vector.
hints: The parallel vector component is \((F\cdot \hat d)\hat d\), and the perpendicular component is what is left over.
problems.setupLatex: Since \(d=\begin{bmatrix}3\\4\end{bmatrix}\), its length is \(5\), so the unit direction is \(\hat d=\begin{bmatrix}3/5\\4/5\end{bmatrix}\).

solutionSteps.label: Find the scalar component
solutionSteps.latex:
The scalar component is
\[F\cdot \hat d=\begin{bmatrix}10\\0\end{bmatrix}\cdot\begin{bmatrix}3/5\\4/5\end{bmatrix}=6.\]

solutionSteps.label: Find the parallel vector component
solutionSteps.latex:
The vector component parallel to \(d\) is
\[(F\cdot \hat d)\hat d=6\begin{bmatrix}3/5\\4/5\end{bmatrix}=\begin{bmatrix}18/5\\24/5\end{bmatrix}.\]

solutionSteps.label: Find the perpendicular vector component
solutionSteps.latex:
Subtract the parallel component from \(F\):
\[F_{\perp}=F-F_{\parallel}=\begin{bmatrix}10\\0\end{bmatrix}-\begin{bmatrix}18/5\\24/5\end{bmatrix}=\begin{bmatrix}32/5\\-24/5\end{bmatrix}.\]

solutionSteps.label: Quick check
solutionSteps.latex:
The perpendicular component should be perpendicular to \(d\):
\[\begin{bmatrix}32/5\\-24/5\end{bmatrix}\cdot\begin{bmatrix}3\\4\end{bmatrix}=\frac{96}{5}-\frac{96}{5}=0.\]

problems.finalAnswerLatex: Scalar component: \(6\). Parallel component: \(\begin{bmatrix}18/5\\24/5\end{bmatrix}\). Perpendicular component: \(\begin{bmatrix}32/5\\-24/5\end{bmatrix}\).
commonMistakes: Calling the scalar component a vector.
Forgetting to normalize the direction vector before computing the scalar component.
Subtracting in the wrong order when finding the perpendicular component.

problems.unit: Diagonalization, Projections, Components, and Cross Product
problems.title: Cross Product, Orthogonality, and Area
skills: Use the cross product as a normal vector
problems.examFlavor: Cross product calculation with geometric interpretation
problems.problemLatex:
Let
\[u=\begin{bmatrix}1\\2\\-1\end{bmatrix},\qquad v=\begin{bmatrix}3\\0\\2\end{bmatrix}.\]

(a) Compute \(u\times v\).

(b) Verify that your answer is perpendicular to both \(u\) and \(v\).

(c) Find the area of the parallelogram spanned by \(u\) and \(v\), and the area of the triangle with sides \(u\) and \(v\).
problems.thinkFirst: The cross product gives a vector perpendicular to both input vectors. Its magnitude gives parallelogram area.
hints: To check perpendicularity, dot your cross product with \(u\) and with \(v\).
Triangle area is half of parallelogram area.
problems.setupLatex: We compute \(u\times v\) component-by-component, then use its norm for area.

solutionSteps.label: Compute the cross product
solutionSteps.latex:
We get
\[u\times v=\begin{bmatrix}2(2)-(-1)(0)\\(-1)(3)-1(2)\\1(0)-2(3)\end{bmatrix}=\begin{bmatrix}4\\-5\\-6\end{bmatrix}.\]

solutionSteps.label: Verify perpendicularity
solutionSteps.latex:
Check with dot products:
\[u\cdot(u\times v)=1(4)+2(-5)+(-1)(-6)=4-10+6=0,\]
and
\[v\cdot(u\times v)=3(4)+0(-5)+2(-6)=12-12=0.\]
So \(u\times v\) is perpendicular to both \(u\) and \(v\).

solutionSteps.label: Compute areas
solutionSteps.latex:
The parallelogram area is
\[\|u\times v\|=\sqrt{4^2+(-5)^2+(-6)^2}=\sqrt{77}.\]
The triangle area is half of that:
\[\frac{\sqrt{77}}{2}.\]

commonMistakes: Swapping \(u	imes v\) and \(v	imes u\), which changes the sign.
Using the dot product instead of the cross product for area.
Forgetting to halve the parallelogram area for the triangle.
problems.similarProblemPrompt: Try computing \(v	imes u\) and compare it to \(u	imes v\).

problems.unit: Diagonalization, Projections, Components, and Cross Product
problems.title: Equation of a Plane Through Three Points
skills: Use two direction vectors in a plane
Find a normal vector with a cross product
problems.problemLatex:
Find an equation of the plane through the points
\[A=(1,0,2),\qquad B=(3,-1,1),\qquad C=(0,2,4).\]
problems.thinkFirst: A plane equation needs a point and a normal vector. The normal vector can be found by crossing two vectors that lie in the plane.
hints: Compute \(\overrightarrow{AB}\times\overrightarrow{AC}\) to get a normal vector.
problems.setupLatex:
Two vectors in the plane are
\[\overrightarrow{AB}=B-A=\begin{bmatrix}2\\-1\\-1\end{bmatrix},\qquad \overrightarrow{AC}=C-A=\begin{bmatrix}-1\\2\\2\end{bmatrix}.\]

solutionSteps.label: Find a normal vector
solutionSteps.latex:
Compute
\[\overrightarrow{AB}\times\overrightarrow{AC}=\begin{bmatrix}2\\-1\\-1\end{bmatrix}\times\begin{bmatrix}-1\\2\\2\end{bmatrix}=\begin{bmatrix}0\\-3\\3\end{bmatrix}.\]
A simpler normal vector is
\[n=\begin{bmatrix}0\\-1\\1\end{bmatrix}.\]

solutionSteps.label: Use point-normal form
solutionSteps.latex:
Using the point \(A=(1,0,2)\), the plane equation is
\[n\cdot\begin{bmatrix}x-1\\y-0\\z-2\end{bmatrix}=0.\]
So
\[0(x-1)-y+(z-2)=0.\]

solutionSteps.label: Simplify
solutionSteps.latex:
The equation becomes
\[-y+z-2=0,\]
or equivalently
\[z-y=2.\]

commonMistakes: Using the coordinates of the three points directly as a determinant without first forming direction vectors.
Forgetting that any nonzero scalar multiple of a normal vector gives the same plane.
Writing a normal vector but not using one of the given points.
problems.similarProblemPrompt: Try finding the plane through \((0,1,1)\), \((2,0,3)\), and \((1,4,2)\).

problems.unit: Diagonalization, Projections, Components, and Cross Product
problems.title: Scalar Triple Product and Volume
problems.examFlavor: Cross product and determinant volume problem
problems.problemLatex:
Let
\[u=\begin{bmatrix}1\\2\\0\end{bmatrix},\qquad v=\begin{bmatrix}-1\\1\\3\end{bmatrix},\qquad w=\begin{bmatrix}2\\0\\1\end{bmatrix}.\]

(a) Compute the scalar triple product \(u\cdot(v\times w)\).

(b) Find the volume of the parallelepiped spanned by \(u\), \(v\), and \(w\).

(c) Are the three vectors coplanar? Explain.
problems.thinkFirst: The scalar triple product gives signed volume. The actual geometric volume is its absolute value.
hints: Compute \(v\times w\) first, then dot with \(u\).
If the scalar triple product is nonzero, the vectors are not coplanar.
problems.setupLatex: We will use \(u\cdot(v\times w)\). Equivalently, this is the determinant of the matrix with columns \(u,v,w\).

solutionSteps.label: Compute \(v	imes w\)
solutionSteps.latex:
First,
\[v\times w=\begin{bmatrix}1(1)-3(0)\\3(2)-(-1)(1)\\(-1)(0)-1(2)\end{bmatrix}=\begin{bmatrix}1\\7\\-2\end{bmatrix}.\]

solutionSteps.label: Dot with \(u\)
solutionSteps.latex:
Now
\[u\cdot(v\times w)=\begin{bmatrix}1\\2\\0\end{bmatrix}\cdot\begin{bmatrix}1\\7\\-2\end{bmatrix}=1+14+0=15.\]

solutionSteps.label: Interpret the result
solutionSteps.latex:
The signed volume is \(15\), so the geometric volume is
\[|15|=15.\]
Because the scalar triple product is nonzero, the vectors are not coplanar.

commonMistakes: Forgetting the absolute value when reporting volume.
Confusing parallelogram area with parallelepiped volume.
Concluding the vectors are coplanar even though the scalar triple product is nonzero.
problems.similarProblemPrompt: Try changing \(w\) to \(u+v\). What happens to the scalar triple product?
